ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302648 covers spring roll as a general category; no specific crispy Vietnamese-style cha gio entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers spring rolls generically. No specific crispy rice-paper Vietnamese variant code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for spring rolls or cha gio as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crispy spring rolls consists of approximately 3 pieces weighing around 120 g, as commonly served as a starter or side dish in Singaporean Vietnamese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Spring rolls, fried, with meat and vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for crispy Vietnamese-style cha gio with crab, prawn, and pork filling was found. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for fried spring rolls with meat and vegetable filling (NDB No. 35193), which is the closest approved analogue. Values reflect deep-fried rice-paper rolls with mixed seafood and pork filling. Adaptation is conservative and consistent with the described ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy Vietnamese-style cha gio. GI of 52 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: fried spring rolls with mixed filling, which have a GI in the low-to-medium range (approximately 50–55) due to the rice paper wrapper (refined starch) moderated by protein and fat from the meat and seafood filling. Reference: Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 25.8) / 100 ≈ 13.5 for a 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~456 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of crispy spring rolls due to the high sodium content from seasoned filling and dipping sauces; a single serving can contribute approximately 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and moderate carbohydrate load from rice paper wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume crispy spring rolls in moderation; the rice paper wrapper contributes refined starch with a GI of approximately 52, and a standard 3-piece serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 13.5, which is within the moderate range and requires portion control and pairing with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from deep-frying and seasoned filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit crispy spring rolls due to the combined effect of deep-frying (contributing saturated and trans fats) and high sodium content, both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional consumption as part of a balanced diet is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Spring Rolls, known in Vietnamese as Chả Giò (Cha Gio), are deep-fried rolls made with a thin rice paper wrapper encasing a savoury filling of crab meat, prawns, minced pork, and finely chopped vegetables such as carrots, mushrooms, and bean sprouts. The rolls are deep-fried in oil until the rice paper wrapper becomes golden, blistered, and exceptionally crispy. The texture contrast between the shatteringly crisp exterior and the moist, flavourful filling is a defining characteristic of this dish. Chả Giò is a staple of Vietnamese cuisine and is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore, often as a starter or side dish accompanied by nuoc cham dipping sauce (a sweet-sour-spicy fish sauce condiment). The dish is nutritionally dense, providing moderate protein from the seafood and pork filling, significant carbohydrate from the rice paper, and elevated fat from deep-frying. The sodium content is notable, particularly when consumed with dipping sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-moderate), moderated by the protein and fat content of the filling. Clinically, the dish warrants portion control for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, fat, and refined carbohydrate content. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or crustacean allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for crispy Vietnamese-style cha gio with crab, prawn, and pork filling was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (fried spring rolls with meat and vegetable filling). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for similar fried rice-paper rolls. Confidence is medium rather than low because the analogue is structurally and compositionally close to the described dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried spring rolls. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried spring rolls. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried spring rolls. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water values are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy analogue and should be treated as approximate. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant (Lorong Telok, Singapore) and is listed on field survey data at SGD 16.48 per order. The description notes that photos are for illustration purposes. Halal status is not confirmed as the filling contains pork. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
